title: How Fiji’s water pipeline ran out of road
gist: Despite Fiji's premium-water brand and tourism image, ~4,200 residents in southern Taveuni have lacked reliable clean water for over a decade as a Salialevu-to-south pipeline is repeatedly announced and never delivered. The piece argues the recurring failure is one of unsustained, uncosted delivery and excluded communities — and a credibility test for Pacific development partners.

insights.claim: More than 4,200 residents in southern Taveuni have been without reliable water for over a decade despite Fiji's extraordinary rainfall, while a Salialevu-to-south pipeline has been announced and re-announced for more than 10 years.
insights.speaker: Mary Watson-Burton
insights.so_what: The gap between national ambition and delivery is the recurring failure mode for Pacific infrastructure; repeated re-announcement is a reliable signal of non-delivery.
insights.locator: A recent Fiji Sun report confirmed

insights.claim: A $3 million desalination plant built by the Water Authority of Fiji at Navaca in 2014 was declared not feasible within a year — diesel-powered and too costly to run — and sits unused, a metaphor for projects that are optimistic but not fully costed or contingency-planned.
insights.speaker: Mary Watson-Burton
insights.so_what: Capex without opex/feasibility planning produces stranded assets; the sustainability plan must be as comprehensive as the build plan — a due-diligence flag for aid-funded infrastructure.
insights.quote: Today it sits unused, a metaphor for development projects that are optimistic but not fully costed
insights.locator: In 2014, the Water Authority of Fiji built

insights.claim: The communities most affected have the least capacity to influence decisions — many southern residents did not know Constitutional Review Commission consultations were happening until they had begun — so projects get built for a community rather than with one.
insights.speaker: Mary Watson-Burton
insights.so_what: Excluding affected communities at the design stage is a structural predictor of project failure; community co-design is a delivery-risk mitigant for development partners.
insights.quote: When communities are not in the room at the design stage, whether that room belongs to government or an NGO, projects are more likely to be built for a community than built with one.
insights.locator: Part of what makes these problems persist

themes.theme: Proof it can work — and the regional stakes

insights.claim: Fiji was validated by the WHO in October 2025 for eliminating trachoma — the 26th country to do so — through sustained investment in clean water, hygiene and community health education, demonstrating delivery is possible when it is sustained, community-grounded and targeted.
insights.speaker: Mary Watson-Burton
insights.so_what: Fiji has demonstrated state delivery capability on hard health challenges, so the Taveuni water failure is a choice of prioritisation, not capacity — relevant to assessing governance quality.
insights.locator: In October 2025, the World Health Organisation validated Fiji

insights.claim: How Fiji resolves the southern Taveuni water challenge will signal not only its capacity to deliver for its own citizens but the credibility of broader regional ambitions for equitable, community-grounded development.
insights.speaker: Mary Watson-Burton
insights.so_what: Last-mile service delivery is the real test of Pacific development credibility; donors and partners should treat end-of-road delivery as the metric, not aggregate participation numbers.
insights.quote: the real test of inclusion and equity lies in whether essential services reach communities at the literal and figurative end of the road
insights.locator: For Fiji, and for development partners across the region
